mnemonics.title: A-B-S-T-R-A-C-T
mnemonics.content: A-B-S-T-R-A-C-T: Abstract Body Shouldn't Totally Reveal Actual Code Technique - Remember that abstract classes hide the implementation details (body) of methods.

mnemonics.title: Abstract Class Rules
mnemonics.content: A-C-C-E-P-T: Abstract Cannot Construct Except to call Parent - Abstract classes can have constructors but you can't instantiate them directly.

acronyms.usage: APIs use abstraction to hide complex implementation from users, exposing only necessary functions.
Abstraction is one of the four pillars of OOP along with Encapsulation, Inheritance, and Polymorphism.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a table with two columns: Abstract Class and Interface. List differences: constructors (yes/no), methods (abstract + concrete / abstract only), inheritance (single/multiple), keywords (abstract / implements).
Imagine a camera lens that blurs everything except the object you're focusing on - abstraction shows only what's essential, hiding the complex details in the background.

analogies.analogy: Think of a vehicle abstract class like a blueprint for all vehicles. It defines that all vehicles have wheels and can move, but the specific implementation (how they move - wheels, tracks, wings) varies for each type.
An interface is like a job description. It defines what a person CAN DO (responsibilities) but not HOW they do it. Multiple people (classes) can have the same job description but perform differently.
When you drive a car, you use the steering wheel, accelerator, and brakes. You don't need to understand how the internal combustion engine or transmission works - that's abstraction at work.
